description: US debt collection defense: FDCPA violations and remedies, debt validation, statutes of limitations by state, garnishment-exempt income, sewer service, and answering collection lawsuits. Verified 2026.
source: FDCPA 15 U.S.C. § 1692 et seq., Regulation F 12 CFR Part 1006, CCPA 15 U.S.C. § 1673, 42 U.S.C. § 407, 31 CFR Part 212, CFPB debt collection guidance, state civil procedure codes

triples.subject: FDCPA (Fair Debt Collection Practices Act)
triples.object: federal law (15 U.S.C. § 1692) regulating third-party debt collectors and debt buyers; generally does not cover original creditors collecting their own debts

triples.subject: FDCPA
triples.object: calls before 8am or after 9pm local time, harassment, obscene language, threats of violence, and false or misleading representations (§§ 1692c-1692e)

triples.subject: FDCPA
triples.object: discussing the debt with third parties like employers or family, except to locate the debtor (§ 1692b-c)

triples.subject: FDCPA damages
triples.object: up to $1,000 statutory damages plus actual damages and attorney fees; consumer must sue within 1 year of the violation (§ 1692k)

triples.subject: Regulation F 7-in-7 rule
triples.object: collector may not call more than 7 times within 7 days about one debt, nor within 7 days of a phone conversation about it (12 CFR 1006.14, effective Nov 2021)

triples.subject: cease communication letter
triples.object: after written demand to stop contact, collector may only confirm receipt or notify of specific action like a lawsuit (§ 1692c(c)); send certified mail, keep a copy

triples.subject: debt validation
triples.object: consumer's right to dispute the debt in writing within 30 days of the initial notice; collector must pause collection until it mails verification (§ 1692g)

triples.subject: debt validation demand
triples.object: debt buyers often cannot produce the original contract or a full chain of assignment; a validation demand or discovery request frequently ends the case

triples.subject: debt buyer
triples.object: purchases charged-off debt for pennies on the dollar (historically averaging around 4 cents per dollar of face value per FTC study) but sues for the full balance

triples.subject: statute of limitations on debt
triples.object: state-law deadline for filing a collection lawsuit, typically 3-6 years for credit card debt, running from the last payment or default; ranges from 3 to 10 years depending on state and contract type

triples.subject: statute of limitations
triples.object: 3 years: New York (consumer credit, CPLR 214-i since 2022), North Carolina, Delaware, Mississippi, South Carolina, New Hampshire

triples.subject: statute of limitations
triples.object: 4 years: California, Texas, Florida (store accounts), Pennsylvania; 5 years: Illinois (unwritten), Missouri, Virginia; 6 years: written contracts in many states including Ohio, Michigan, New Jersey, Washington

triples.subject: statute of limitations revival
triples.object: in many states a partial payment or written acknowledgment of a stale debt restarts the limitations clock; never make a token payment on old debt without checking state law

triples.subject: time-barred debt
triples.object: suing or threatening suit on debt past the statute of limitations violates the FDCPA; Regulation F expressly bans such suits (12 CFR 1006.26)

triples.subject: time-barred debt
triples.object: the debt still exists and can be reported on credit for up to 7 years from first delinquency (FCRA), but courts cannot enforce it if the SOL defense is raised

triples.subject: statute of limitations defense
triples.object: SOL is an affirmative defense; it is waived unless raised in the answer, so ignoring a lawsuit on time-barred debt still produces a valid default judgment

triples.subject: collection lawsuit answer
triples.object: file a written answer by the deadline (often 20-30 days after service) denying what is not proven and raising defenses; most collection suits win by default because 70%+ of defendants never respond

triples.subject: sewer service
triples.object: process server falsely claims to have served the lawsuit (e.g., tosses it or fabricates the affidavit), so the defendant never learns of the case until a default judgment or garnishment appears

triples.subject: sewer service remedy
triples.object: motion to vacate the default judgment for lack of personal jurisdiction / improper service; evidence like work records or a wrong physical description on the affidavit of service helps

triples.subject: wage garnishment
triples.object: capped at the lesser of 25% of disposable earnings or the amount exceeding 30 times the federal minimum wage per week (CCPA, 15 U.S.C. § 1673)

triples.subject: wage garnishment
triples.object: Texas, Pennsylvania, North Carolina, and South Carolina prohibit wage garnishment for most consumer debts; many other states protect more than the federal floor

triples.subject: exempt income
triples.object: Social Security, SSI, VA benefits, and most federal pensions cannot be garnished by judgment creditors for consumer debt (42 U.S.C. § 407)

triples.subject: bank account protection
triples.object: banks must automatically protect two months' worth of directly deposited federal benefits from account freezes and garnishment (31 CFR Part 212)

triples.subject: exempt income
triples.object: protection is strongest when benefits are direct-deposited and not commingled; funds moved to another account may require claiming the exemption in court

triples.subject: debt collection court hearing
triples.object: simply appearing and demanding proof (original signed contract, complete account statements, assignment chain) causes many debt-buyer cases to be dismissed or settled for far less

triples.subject: identity of collector
triples.object: verify the caller: get the company name, mailing address, and account details in writing before paying anything; fake-debt scams mimic collectors and demand gift cards or wires

triples.subject: CFPB complaint
triples.object: consumerfinance.gov/complaint or (855) 411-2372; companies must respond, and complaints create a record for later litigation; state attorney general is a parallel channel

triples.subject: legal help for debt defense
triples.object: Legal Services Corporation offices (lsc.gov), state legal aid, law school clinics; NACA (consumeradvocates.org) lists private consumer attorneys who take FDCPA cases on contingency because fees are statutory
